episodeOutline.title: First Bell
episodeOutline.synopsis: It's the first day at Ridgeview Academy, and every hallway, lunch table, and group project feels like a test. As the new kid, you'll decide how loud or quiet to be, who to sit with, and what to do when someone else gets caught in the crossfire of popularity. By the final bell, you'll have started to answer a bigger question: do you want to fit in, or do you want to belong?

scenes.title: New Building, Same Nerves
scenes.description: The player steps through the doors of Ridgeview Academy for the first time, schedule crumpled in hand, trying to look like they've done this a thousand times before.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Anticipatory anxiety, the universal 'first day' feeling

scenes.title: All Eyes On You
scenes.description: Ms. Alvarez welcomes the player and asks them to introduce themselves to the class. Twenty-something strangers turn to look, including Jordan Reyes in the back row and a quiet girl, Priya Chen, sketching in the corner.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Spotlight pressure - the first small test of who you're going to be here

scenes.title: Owning the Room
scenes.description: The player introduces themselves with energy and a genuine detail about their life. A few kids laugh warmly; Jordan gives an approving nod from the back.
scenes.emotionalBeat: A small rush of validation, but also exposure

scenes.title: Quick and Quiet
scenes.description: The player keeps it short and sits down fast, cheeks warm. Priya glances up from her sketchbook, a flicker of recognition in her eyes.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Relief mixed with a nagging sense of invisibility

scenes.title: Breaking the Ice
scenes.description: The player tosses in a self-deprecating joke about getting lost twice already. The class laughs, Jordan laughs loudest, and Priya smiles down at her sketchbook.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Relief through humor, a fragile but real connection

scenes.title: The Cafeteria Gauntlet
scenes.description: Tray in hand, the player scans the room. Jordan waves them over to the loudest, most crowded table. Across the room, Priya sits alone, headphones in, sketching quietly.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The classic crossroads - visibility versus connection

scenes.title: The Popular Table
scenes.description: The player sits with Jordan's crowd. The conversation is fast, funny, and a little cutting - jokes at other students' expense included.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Belonging that feels conditional

scenes.title: The Quiet Corner
scenes.description: The player sits across from Priya. After a slow start, she shows the player her sketchbook - detailed, imaginative drawings of imaginary creatures.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Small, unforced intimacy - a real conversation forming

scenes.title: A Table for One
scenes.description: The player picks an empty table, telling themselves it's fine to just observe today. It's quieter than expected, and lonelier too.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Self-protection that curdles into isolation

scenes.title: Partnered Up
scenes.description: Ms. Alvarez announces a group project and pairs the player with Priya. Depending on lunch, this either feels natural or awkward.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Forced proximity as a chance for growth or friction

scenes.title: My Way
scenes.description: The player decides the project direction without much input from Priya, who quietly goes along with it, her sketchbook closed.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Efficient but hollow - a missed connection

scenes.title: Building Together
scenes.description: The player asks Priya what she's imagining first. Her idea - turning the assignment into an illustrated story - lights up as they build it together.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Genuine collaboration, mutual respect forming

scenes.title: Let Her Lead
scenes.description: The player stays quiet and lets Priya make every decision, unsure of their own opinions and afraid of getting it wrong.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Safety through passivity, but a nagging sense of not showing up

scenes.title: Caught in the Crossfire
scenes.description: Between classes, Jordan spots Priya's sketchbook and reads a page aloud in a mocking voice to a small crowd. Priya freezes, face reddening. Everyone looks at the player.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The core moral pressure point of the episode

scenes.title: Speaking Up
scenes.description: The player steps in, telling Jordan to knock it off - calmly but firmly. The crowd goes quiet. Priya looks at the player like she's seeing them for the first time.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Courage that costs something but earns real trust

scenes.title: Laughing Along
scenes.description: The player laughs along with the crowd to avoid becoming a target themselves. Priya's eyes flick to the player, and something closes off in her expression.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The uncomfortable cost of self-preservation

scenes.title: Changing the Subject
scenes.description: The player cracks a joke that shifts the crowd's attention away from Priya entirely, defusing the moment without direct confrontation. Priya slips away, unsure how to read what just happened.
scenes.emotionalBeat: A clever escape that leaves things unresolved

scenes.title: Finishing Touches
scenes.description: The player and Priya meet to finalize the project presentation. Priya suggests including her original illustrated story idea, unsure if it's 'too much' for the assignment.
scenes.emotionalBeat: A quiet moment of decision about what kind of work - and person - to present tomorrow

scenes.title: Going With the Real Thing
scenes.description: The player encourages using Priya's original idea, even if it's different from what other groups are doing. Priya's relief is visible.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Choosing authenticity over safety

scenes.title: Playing It Safe
scenes.description: The player suggests copying the format other, more popular groups are using instead. Priya nods, but quietly closes her sketchbook.
scenes.emotionalBeat: A small betrayal of something real for the sake of fitting in

scenes.title: Front of the Class
scenes.description: The project is presented. However it goes, the day is almost over - and the player has to decide, walking out of class, what tomorrow is going to look like.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The culmination of a single day's worth of small choices

scenes.title: Someone to Sit With
scenes.description: Walking out, the player catches up with Priya and asks about tomorrow's lunch. She smiles - genuinely, for the first time all day - and says she'd like that.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Belonging built on being seen, not performed

scenes.title: Sitting Pretty
scenes.description: The player leaves with Jordan's group, laughing at the right moments, already invited to sit at the popular table tomorrow. It feels good. It also feels like a costume.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Belonging that costs a piece of yourself

scenes.title: Walking Out Alone
scenes.description: The player walks out alone, telling themselves tomorrow will be easier once people know them. Maybe it will be. Today, though, no one waves goodbye.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The quiet ache of a day spent protecting yourself instead of showing up

choicePoints.prompt: Ms. Alvarez asks you to introduce yourself to the class. Every face is turned toward you.
choicePoints.context: The first impression you make will ripple through the rest of the day.

options.text: Stand tall and share something real and interesting about yourself
Keep it brief and sit down as quickly as possible
Crack a self-deprecating joke to get a laugh

choicePoints.prompt: Jordan waves you over to the loudest table in the cafeteria. Across the room, Priya sits alone, headphones in.
choicePoints.context: Where you sit today might quietly decide who you sit with for the rest of the year.

options.text: Join Jordan's table - it's the easy, visible choice
Sit alone and just watch today

choicePoints.prompt: Ms. Alvarez pairs you with Priya for the group project. Time to figure out how you two are going to work together.
choicePoints.context: The way you handle a partner you didn't necessarily expect says a lot about you.

options.text: Take charge and decide the direction yourself
Ask what she's imagining first, and build from there together
Stay quiet and let her make all the decisions

choicePoints.prompt: Jordan is reading Priya's private sketchbook aloud in a mocking voice while a crowd gathers. Priya looks mortified. Everyone's waiting to see what you'll do.
choicePoints.context: This is the moment the whole day has been building toward - the choice with real social risk.

options.text: Step in and tell Jordan to stop
Laugh along so you don't become the next target
Crack a joke that redirects everyone's attention away from Priya

choicePoints.prompt: Priya wonders aloud if her illustrated story idea is 'too much' for the project. You have to decide what to tell her - and what to actually present tomorrow.
choicePoints.context: This choice is really about whether you value being interesting or being safe.

options.text: Encourage her to go with her real idea, differences and all
Suggest copying the format other, more popular groups are using

choicePoints.prompt: Walking out after the presentation, you have to decide what kind of first year you actually want.
choicePoints.context: One day is ending, but it's already shaping what tomorrow looks like.

options.text: Catch up with Priya and ask about tomorrow
Head off with Jordan's group, already invited to sit with them tomorrow
Walk out alone, telling yourself tomorrow will be easier

branches.name: The Genuine Connection Path
branches.description: Player consistently prioritizes real connection with Priya over social visibility, ending in earned belonging.

branches.name: The Popularity Chase Path
branches.description: Player chases visibility and status at the cost of empathy, ending in a hollow but socially secure position.

branches.name: The Self-Protective Path
branches.description: Player avoids risk and confrontation throughout, ending isolated despite good intentions.

characterArcs.arc: From invisible and guarded to either genuinely seen (if player chooses connection/defense) or further withdrawn (if player chooses safety/neutrality).
Remains largely constant across paths - a charismatic but sometimes careless figure - functioning as a mirror for the player's own choices about status versus kindness.
Moves from uncertain outsider through a series of self-defining choices toward one of three distinct identities: authentic connector, status-seeker, or self-protective observer.

hiddenIn: whether you take charge or defer on the project
whether you speak up in the hallway
who you choose to sit with at lunch
how you treat your project partner's ideas
how you respond when someone else is mocked
how you handle an unplanned partner pairing
whether you defuse or escalate the hallway conflict
how you respond to social pressure while finishing the project

relationshipDynamics.dynamic: Can range from a genuine early friendship to a missed connection, depending almost entirely on small choices around attention and defense.
Can range from an ally you've disappointed, to a friend you've impressed, to someone whose approval you've simply stopped needing.

replayHooks: Three distinct endings (belonging, popularity, isolation) invite replay to see all outcomes
Priya's sketchbook and hidden creative idea only fully pays off on the empathy-forward path, rewarding a second playthrough
Jordan's mockery scene reads completely differently depending on whether the player sat with him at lunch
The 'safe vs authentic' presentation choice changes the tone of the final classroom scene even on a repeat path
Players who chase popularity may want to replay to see what genuine connection with Priya feels like, and vice versa
educationalGoals: Model that confidence can be expressed in different ways (loud, quiet, humorous) without one being 'more correct'
Show that empathy often requires a small, specific cost (social risk) rather than being effortless
Illustrate adaptability as responding well to unexpected pairings and pressure, not just going along with everything
Demonstrate that authenticity and belonging are earned through consistent small choices, not a single grand gesture
conversationStarters: Would you have sat with Priya or with Jordan's table? Why?
Was staying neutral in the hallway a betrayal, or just self-protection - can it be both?
Which ending felt most true to how you actually act on a first day somewhere new?
Did Priya deserve more from the player earlier in the story, regardless of what happened in the hallway?
What would it take for the 'popularity' ending to actually feel like belonging?

designNotes: The episode uses a single recurring relationship (Priya) as the emotional throughline, so that every choice - even ones that seem separate, like where to sit at lunch or how to introduce yourself - accumulates meaning by the hallway confrontation. Jordan is intentionally not a villain; he's mostly thoughtless rather than cruel, so that 'staying neutral' or 'joining his table' remain genuinely tempting choices rather than obviously wrong ones. This keeps the moral texture honest rather than preachy. Trait mapping is embedded entirely in mundane school choices (seating, group work, hallway reactions) so no scene ever explicitly references confidence, empathy, or adaptability.
uncertainties: Whether three full endings is the right number for a 12-15 minute episode, or if collapsing to two would create tighter replay value
Whether Jordan needs a redemptive beat later in the season to avoid feeling like a static 'popularity' stand-in across repeat plays
Whether the 'sit alone' lunch option is distinct enough from 'stay neutral' later, or if they read as redundant expressions of the same avoidance instinct
Whether the hallway mockery scene is intense enough to feel like a real moral stakes moment without additional buildup earlier in the episode
alternativesConsidered: A version where the choice point order was reversed (hallway conflict first, lunch seating second) to front-load the moral stakes - rejected because it removed the slow-build tension of getting to know Priya before the crisis point
Adding a fourth ending focused purely on ADAPTABILITY ('the chameleon') who mirrors whoever they're with - considered but cut to avoid diluting the three cleaner trait-forward endings

Making Jordan the player's assigned project partner instead of Priya, to raise stakes with the more socially powerful character - rejected because it removed the quieter, lower-stakes empathy beats that make the hallway moment land emotionally
